title: ST558_HW7
text:
Reading in the Data
First, load the libraries needed for importing and working with the dataset.

library(readr)
library(dplyr)

Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.5.3



Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

library(ggplot2)

Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.5.3

library(janitor)

Warning: package 'janitor' was built under R version 4.5.3



Attaching package: 'janitor'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    chisq.test, fisher.test


Next, read the CSV file into R using the read_csv() function from the readr package.

bike <- read_csv(
  "Data/SeoulBikeData.csv",
  locale = locale(encoding = "Windows-1252"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  clean_names()

# convert few of variables to factors
bike <- bike |>
  mutate(
    date = as.Date(date, format = "%d/%m/%Y"),
    across(
      c(seasons, holiday, functioning_day),
      as.factor
    )
  )

Finally, inspect the data to verify that it was imported correctly.

head(bike)

# A tibble: 6 × 14
  date       rented_bike_count  hour temperature_c humidity_percent
  <date>                 <dbl> <dbl>         <dbl>            <dbl>

3 2017-12-01               173     2          -6                 39
4 2017-12-01               107     3          -6.2               40
5 2017-12-01                78     4          -6                 36
6 2017-12-01               100     5          -6.4               37
# ℹ 9 more variables: wind_speed_m_s <dbl>, visibility_10m <dbl>,
#   dew_point_temperature_c <dbl>, solar_radiation_mj_m2 <dbl>,

glimpse(bike)

The dataset is stored in the object bike, which will be used throughout the analysis. The head() function displays the first few rows, glimpse() provides the structure and variable types, and summary() gives descriptive statistics for each variable.
